era_context: The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander (r. 222–235) faces mounting pressure on multiple frontiers. The Sassanid Empire is founded in 224 CE under Ardashir I, who has just overthrown the Parthian Arsacid dynasty; 226 marks the early consolidation of Sassanid power. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well-established; Wei dominates the north under Cao Pi (r. 220–226), while Wu and Shu maintain independent states. This is an era of imperial transition, frontier warfare, and political consolidation across Eurasia.

events.title: Cao Pi dies; Cao Rui succeeds as Wei emperor
events.description: Cao Pi (r. 220–226), the founder of the Wei Dynasty and son of Cao Cao, dies in 226 CE. He is succeeded by his son Cao Rui (r. 226–239). Cao Pi's reign saw the formal establishment of Wei as a dynasty and the consolidation of northern China under Wei control. His death marks a succession transition, though it is smooth and well-documented. Cao Rui's succession ensures continuity of Wei's dominance in northern China. Cao Pi's reign is significant for establishing the institutional foundations of Wei that will persist through Cao Rui's reign.
key_figures: Cao Cao (Cao Pi's father, founder of Wei, died 220)
events.certainty_note: Cao Pi's death in 226 CE and Cao Rui's succession are firmly established in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Follows from 220-001 (Cao Pi becomes Wei emperor)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation under Ardashir I
events.description: Ardashir I (r. 224–241), founder of the Sassanid Empire, continues consolidating power two years after overthrowing the Parthian Arsacid dynasty in 224 CE. By 226, Sassanid control is established over Persia and Mesopotamia. Ardashir's consolidation includes administrative reorganization, military strengthening, and the establishment of Zoroastrianism as the state religion. The Sassanid Empire emerges as a major power rivaling Rome. Ardashir's reign establishes the institutional foundations of the Sassanid Empire that will persist for four centuries.
key_figures: Papak (Ardashir's father, founder of Sassanid dynasty)

sources.name: Sassanid inscriptions (Res Gestae Divi Saporis, later compilation)
Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Ardashir I's consolidation of the Sassanid Empire by 226 is well-attested in multiple source traditions, though details vary.

events.title: Sun Quan consolidates Wu state
events.description: Sun Quan (r. 222–252), the second emperor of Wu, continues consolidating his state's administrative and military structures. Wu controls the Yangtze River valley and southern coastal regions, giving it economic advantages despite military pressure from Wei. Sun Quan's reign sees the development of Wu's naval power and administrative systems. By 226, Wu is well-established as a viable competitor to Wei despite the latter's northern dominance. Sun Quan's consolidation ensures Wu's survival as an independent state through the remainder of the Three Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Sun Ce (Sun Quan's father, founder of Wu, died 226)
events.certainty_note: Sun Quan's consolidation of Wu is well-attested in Chinese historical records for this period.
cross_references: Related to 226-001 (Cao Pi's death and Wei succession)

events.title: Roman-Sassanid frontier tensions escalate
events.description: Tensions between Rome and the newly consolidated Sassanid Empire escalate in 226. Ardashir I's consolidation of Sassanid power and his ambitions to expand westward into Mesopotamia create conflict with Rome. Severus Alexander's Roman Empire faces the challenge of a new, aggressive eastern power. The exact sequence of military operations is unclear from surviving sources, but this period marks the beginning of centuries of Sassanid-Roman conflict. Both empires begin military preparations for larger conflicts.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Escalation of Roman-Sassanid tensions in 226 is probable given Ardashir's consolidation, but specific military operations are not clearly dated to this year in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Sassanid-Roman military escalation to 226 vs. 227–230 is uncertain. Some sources suggest that major military operations did not begin until 230 or later. The extent of Ardashir I's territorial consolidation by 226 is also debated; some scholars argue that full Sassanid control of Mesopotamia took longer to establish. For Chinese history, the dating of Sun Quan's consolidation is well-attested, but some details of administrative reforms are uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 226 CE is moderate. Chinese sources for Cao Pi's death and Sun Quan's consolidation are well-preserved and reliable. Roman and Sassanid sources for frontier tensions are less clear; Herodian is non-contemporary (writing c. 240 CE), and the Historia Augusta is unreliable. Sassanid sources are sparse and often later inscriptions. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Regional coverage is skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and China; Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are largely absent. The bias toward elite political narratives is pronounced.

graph_edges.note: Cao Rui's succession follows from Cao Pi's death
Ardashir's consolidation follows from Sassanid founding
Roman-Sassanid tensions escalate due to Ardashir's consolidation
Escalating tensions lead to major military conflict
Wei and Wu succession/consolidation occur simultaneously
